Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect code generation for blocks connected to variant or function-call subsystem
Description: The C code generated from a Simulink model might be incorrect under all of the following conditions: The model contains a variant subsystem or function-call subsystem that is specified as inlined  (or auto , but is inlined in the generated code). For the function-call subsystem, it must be called from a user-written S-Function block. The variant or function-call subsystem contains an Inport block that is directly connected to an Outport block. In this case, the Inport block is non-virtual, which is indicated by an execution number. To see the execution number, on the model diagram window, enable Format > Block Displays > Sorted Order .  The generated C code might not include the code for the output signal of the Inport block.
Workaround: To avoid nonvirtual subsystem input ports, insert a Signal Conversion block at the output of the Inport block. This change does not affect the efficiency of the generated C code.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Generated functions SetDims or FinalizeDims might access NULL pointers for referenced model with variable dimension signal
Description: When a model has a variable dimension signal, the generated SetDims  or FinalizeDims  functions might access NULL  pointers when all of the following conditions are true:  The model is a referenced model. The model parameter Total number of instances allowed per top model  is set to One .  The referenced model contains a subsystem, where the subsystem parameter Function packaging  is set to Function .  ?
Workaround: To work around this issue, do either of the following:Set the model parameter Total number of instances allowed per top model to Multiple.Change the subsystem whose Function packaging parameter is set to Function packaging to either Reusable function or Inline. ?

Summary: Code generation of a model might crash MATLAB
Description: Generating code for a model might crash MATLAB under the following conditions: The model contains a Stateflow chart or MATLAB block that uses local data with an initialization value The value of the model configuration parameter Maximum stack size (bytes) , on the Optimization > Signals and Parameters  pane, is set too low.
Workaround: Open the Configuration Parameters dialog box and on the Optimization > Signals and Parameters pane increase the value of the Maximum stack size (bytes) parameter.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Models that reference other models that contain either Sample Time Math blocks or masked blocks that contain SampleTime Math blocks can produce incorrect simulation results
Description: If a model that contains either a Sample Time Math block or a masked block that contains a Sample Time Math block (e.g. the Discrete Derivative block) is referenced by another model, then the software hard-codes the sample time of the Sample Time Math block into the generated code of the referenced model. If you reference this model when the 'Model' block of the parent model has a sample time that is different from that of the Sample Time Math block in the referenced model, then the simulation produces incorrect results.

Summary: Lookup Table blocks might produce unnecessary signal overflow warning or error messages at run time
Description: When ALL of the following conditions are true, the n-D Lookup Table and Interpolation Using Prelookup blocks might produce an unnecessary signal overflow warning or error message at run time: Input data type and output data type are fixed-point. Table data  data type and Intermediate results  data type are the same and don't have any bias. Table data  data type is unsigned. The model configuration parameter, Detect overflow  (Configuration Parameters >  Diagnostics > Data Validity > Signals), is set to "warning" or "error".

Summary: Bus Editor can be unresponsive after model compilation
Description: Clicking an open Bus Editor after model compilation results in the Bus Editor reloading itself, which causes MATLAB to be slow and unresponsive.
Workaround: Install the appropriate attached .zip file, depending on the release that you are using.Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.Exit MATLAB.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. To see the contents of the .zip file: Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh):unzip -l <MATLAB>/<.zip file>Extract the files from the .zip file. Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh): cd <MATLAB>unzip <.zip file>Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.*Notes for Windows Systems:If you see ˇ°Error: Access is deniedˇ± when attempting to extract the .zip file, you may need to start ˇ°WinZipˇ± in administrator mode. Do this by navigating to WinZip.exe or the WinZip Start menu entry, right-clicking on it, and selecting ˇ°Run as administratorˇ±.From within WinZip, find and open the downloaded .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.

Summary: Callback actions on Stateflow annotations sometimes cause crashes
Description: Stateflow might become unstable if after you trigger a callback from an annotation, the callback action takes focus away from the Stateflow Editor and then you close the editor without focusing on a Stateflow chart. The symptom is a stack trace containing the line: failed assertion in .\utils\object.cpp at line 2853: object[id]!=NULL

Summary: When simulated in Rapid Accelerator mode or Rsim target, a model with more than one variable sample time and a fixed sample time might incorrectly return extra time steps
Description: When simulated in Rapid Accelerator mode or the Rsim target, a model with more than one variable sample time and a fixed sample time might incorrectly return extra time steps. In cases where you expect to see a single sample time, you might see multiple sample times that are very close together (with the distance between them being on the order of your machine precision). ?

Summary: Simulink "Conditional input branch execution" optimization produces wrong answers for subsystem with constant sample time blocks
Description: Simulink produces wrong answers and generates wrong code if a model meets all  of the following conditions: The Configuration Parameters > Optimization > Conditional input branch execution  setting is enabled. The model contains a Switch block. A subsystem containing constant (Inf ) sample time blocks is directly connected to one of the data input ports (input port 1 and 3) of a Switch block. The blocks inside the subsystem are conditionally executed by the Switch block. You can identify such blocks by displaying the sorted execution orders (turn on Format > Block Displays > Sorted Order ). For example, if the Switch block displays as 2:5{3,4}  and the subsystem displays as 3:0{6} , 3:0  means that the execution order of the subsystem is first inside the conditional execution context {3}  set up by the Switch block input. Inside the subsystem, there is a constant sample time block and a virtual subsystem block, and the constant sample time block feeds into another constant sample time block inside the virtual subsystem.
Workaround: Clear the Configuration Parameters > Optimization > Conditional input branch execution parameter.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: n-D Lookup Table block: code generation and simulation results might not match, or code generation might error out when index search method is "evenly spaced points"
Description: Suppose that your model contains an n-D Lookup Table block, where all of the following conditions are satisfied: Inline parameters is "on". Index search method is "evenly spaced points". Breakpoint data type is integer or fixed point. The corresponding input port data type is floating point. The breakpoints have power-of-2 even spacing.  If the power is zero, code generation and simulation results do not match. If the power is greater than zero, code generation errors out.
Workaround: Try one of the following workarounds:In the Configuration Parameters dialog box, you can go to the Optimization pane and clear the Inline parameters check box.You can set the breakpoint data type to be the same as the corresponding input port data type.

Summary: Model configured for AUTOSAR code generation cannot use a custom storage class that is a reference to PerInstanceMemory
Description: Update diagram fails for a model that is configured for AUTOSAR code generation if both of the the following conditions exist: The model uses a Simulink.Signal  data object. The Simulink.Signal  object is configured with a custom storage class that is a reference to the PerInstanceMemory  custom storage class.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Enabled subsystems connected to Matrix Concatenate block may produce incorrect code
Description: The model output may be incorrect when the following conditions are present: The output of a conditionally executed subsystem must be connected to the 2nd or higher input of a vector or matrix concat block The output port block should have the check box "Reset when disabled" selected The output port block is connected to a model reference, reusable atomic subsystem, s-function or a block with a matching TLC file in the rtw/c/tlc/blocks folder  The output is incorrect because it is not reset correctly in the Matrix Concatenate block output. It writes the wrong elements to the Matrix Concatenate block output. ?
Workaround: Force the output of the subsystem to be separate from the Matrix Concatenate output. For example, place a test point on the output of the subsystem.Replace the referenced model with a subsystem.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect code generation with data store mappings
Description: When a Stateflow atomic subchart is reused multiple times with different data store mappings, then some of the reused instances might access the wrong data stores. In such cases, the generated code will not match the simulation results.
Workaround: Set the "Code generation function packaging" of the offending atomic subchart to "Inline".

Summary: For models with a root-level control port and Data Store Read or Write blocks, running the Model Advisor check, "Check for potential ordering issues involving data store access" causes Simulink to hang
Description: If a model has a root-level control port, such as an Enable port, a Trigger port, or a Function-Call port, and has a Data Store Read or Data Store Write block, running the Model Advisor check, "Check for potential ordering issues involving data store access" causes Simulink to hang.

Summary: Loading data for root input ports in MATLAB timeseries format produces incorrect results if the timeseries IsTimeFirst property differs from the default value
Description: The MATLAB timeseries Data  property allows the time dimension to be first or last. For example, you can represent a 3x2 signal with 200 time points in either of these ways:  3x2x200 (IsTimeFirst = 0) 200x3x2 (IsTimeFirst = 1)  Simulink requires the time dimension to be as follows:  If the signal is a scalar or a 1-D vector, time must be the first dimension. If the signal has more than 1 dimension, time must be the last dimension.  Loading data for a timeseries that does not meet these conditions produces incorrect results.  When created or loaded from a file, a timeseries object that does not meet these requirements result in  a warning at the MATLAB prompt.  >> load sample_timeseries.mat Warning: In future releases the isTimeFirst property of this timeseries will be calculated based on the data and will differ from its current representation.
Workaround: To fix this problem, use the permute function to change the Data property of the timeseries such that: If the signal is a scalar or a 1-D vector, time is the first dimension.If the signal has more than 1 dimension, time is the last dimension.

Summary: Build error for AUTOSAR multirunnable model if outport has enumerated data type and Underspecified initialization detection is set to Simplified
Description: Code generation fails for an AUTOSAR multirunnable model if both of the following conditions exist: The Simulink diagnostic Underspecified initialization detection  (UnderspecifiedInitializationDetection ) is set to Simplified . An outport within the runnable systems has an enumerated data type.  Under these conditions, the build process might display an error message similar to the following: When exporting runnables, the 'Initial output' value of block 'myModel/mySWC/Port1'' must be set to '0', '[]'. However, attempting to set the Initial output  value to an enumerated zero value or []  does not remedy the situation and can lead to further errors.
Products: Embedded IDE Link, Target Support Package, Embedded Coder

Summary: The logged datatype for states with an enum datatype may be incorrect for states in referenced models
Description: When a model logs states, the logged state may have an incorrect datatype if all  of these conditions exist: There is a state with an enum  datatype. The state is contained in a referenced model. The referenced model is directly or indirectly referenced from a Model block that has the Simulation mode  parameter set to Normal .  In this case, for the states with an enum  datatype, the variable that is created in the base workspace may have values with the wrong datatype.
Workaround: For all Model blocks that directly or indirectly reference a models that contain states with enum datatype, set the Simulation mode parameter to Accelerator.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Signal connected to Unit Delay or Memory block on both sides of a subsystem boundary
Description: The generated code is incorrect when a model contains all of the following: A signal, with two or more destination blocks, crosses a subsystem boundary. The signal is implemented as a single global buffer through specification. The signal is an input to either a Unit Delay or a Memory block on both sides of the subsystem boundary. The Unit Delay or Memory blocks have an initial condition of zero.  For an example, see the attached model mIncorrectStateReuse.mdl . Specifically, please notice that the state of Unit Delay1  in Subsystem1  is optimized away incorrectly.
Workaround: Turn off the code generation optimization that causes the incorrect code by doing one of the following:To turn off the optimization globally, download the attached MATLAB script WorkaroundForBugReport760112.p. To enable the workaround, execute the script WorkaroundForBugReport760112(true); to disable the workaround, execute the script WorkaroundForBugReport760112(false).To turn off the optimization locally, insert a Signal Conversion block at the input of the Unit Delay or Memory block that triggers the bug.Detect the Unit Delay or Memory block usage that could trigger the bug by doing the following:Download the attached MATLAB script DetectForBugReport760112.p;Run the script on the model DetectForBugReport760112(model);Verify that the code generated for the detected block is correct.

Summary: AUTOSAR code generation fails if the length of a runnable name exceeds 50 characters
Description: As part of configuring a model for AUTOSAR code generation, you provide names for runnable entities. If the length of a specified runnable name exceeds 50 characters, code generation fails and does not generate AUTOSAR files. The build process displays a warning about the name length, similar to the following: Warning: File: writeCodeInfoFcn.m Line: 85 Column: 9 'SampleClient_diag_diagApplicationLongLongLongLongLongLongLongNamed_Interface'exceeds the MATLAB maximum name length of 63 characters and will be truncated to'SampleClient_diag_diagApplicationLongLongLongLongLongLongLongNa'.

Summary: Code generation for signal with asynchronous sample time and non-auto storage class causes SEGV
Description: Code generation for a model causes MATLAB to crash under the following conditions: The model contains a root output port with an asynchronous sample time The signal connected to this root output port has a non-auto storage class defined The model contains a conditionally executed subsystem with a subsystem Output Port block that has a non-empty initialization value
Workaround: Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation.Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.Exit MATLAB.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. To see the contents of the .zip file: Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.Extract the files from the .zip file. Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.*Notes for Windows Systems:If you see ˇ°Error: Access is deniedˇ± when attempting to extract the .zip file, you may need to start ˇ°WinZipˇ± in administrator mode. Do this by navigating to WinZip.exe or the WinZip Start menu entry, right-clicking on it, and selecting ˇ°Run as administratorˇ±.From within WinZip, find and open the downloaded .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.Change log:December 6, 2011: attachment_759421_10b_win32_2011-11-11.zip: initial version, works on R2010b win32 only?

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Constant block of bus data type and value 0 may cause incorrect simulation results or MATLAB crash
Description: Simulating or generating code for a model that satisfies the following conditions may cause incorrect simulation results, MATLAB crash, or incorrect code generation. The model contains a Constant block of bus data type. The Constant value  parameter of this block is 0 .
Workaround: If you want to specify the Constant value parameter of a Constant block to be the ground (zero representation) value of the block's output bus data type, use a MATLAB structure instead of specifying the scalar value 0.Use the function Simulink.Bus.createMATLABStruct to create a MATLAB structure from a bus object.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: For tunable parameter expression
Description: When a tunable block parameter expression contains a cast, the corresponding generated code omits the cast, which can lead to behavior that is inconsistent with simulation. For example, if the tunable block parameter expression is 1.0 - double(A > 50.0) then the generated code is 1.0 - modelP.A > 50.0) This leads to incorrect results because the minus operator has higher precedence than the comparison operator.
Workaround: When a tunable block parameter expression contains a cast, surround the operand of the cast () operator with extra parentheses.For example, if the tunable block parameter expression is1.0 - double(A > 50.0)modify this expression to be1.0 - double((A > 50.0))The generated code is1.0 - (real_T)(modelP.A > 50.0))

Summary: Incorrect error message issued when referencing a triggered model
Description: Simulink issues an incorrect error message when handling a reference to a model that meets all of the following conditions : Has a root level trigger port with the Trigger type  parameter set to rising , falling  or either  Has a root level trigger port with the Triger signal sample time  parameter set to -1  Uses a variable-step solver  The incorrect error is similar to the following: Root Inport 'refModel/In1' has a sample time of [0, 0] which does not match the sample time [0, 1] of its destination: input port 1 of block 'refModel/Sum'. Root inports must have the same sample time as their destinations for model reference. Please correct this situation by changing one of the two sample times or inserting a Rate Transition block ?
Workaround: On the root level trigger port, set the Trigger signal sample time  to 0, or to any discrete sample time.

Summary: MATLAB Function block accessing global data store from referenced model may cause simulation error
Description: Simulink may generate a simulation error if all the following conditions are met: Your model references another model whose Simulation mode  is set to Accelerator . The referenced model contains a MATLAB Function block. The MATLAB Function block reads from or writes to a global data store.  The error message has the following form: MATLAB Function Interface Error: Block 'sub/MATLAB Function/ SFunction ' is calling ssReadFromDataStoreElement with an element index of (-1); element indices must be non-negative  In some cases, the error may be invalid. You can determine whether the errror is invalid as follows: Set the Simulation mode  of the referenced model to Normal . Simulate the parent model. If the error does not appear, it was invalid.
Workaround: Restructure the algorithm in the MATLAB Function block if the following two conditions are met:The MATLAB Function block references the global data store through a condition in an if statementThe condition utilizes a short circuit operator such as && or ||Restructure the algorithm as follows:Replace && operators with nested if statements.Replace || operators with duplicate code.To prevent optimizations that might cause the original error, replace the second argument for the replaced && or || operator with a function that cannot be inlined.For example, consider the following MATLAB algorithm, where X and Y are global data store memory blocks. This algorithm utilizes the && operator.if (X > 0) && (Y(X) < 3)  k = k + 1;endYou can restructure this algorithm by using nested if statements and a noninlined functionif (X > 0)  if Y_X_less_than_3()    k = k + 1;  endend...function result = Y_X_less_than_3()  coder.inline('never') %% don't inline  result = Y(X) < 3;endSimilarly, consider the following MATLAB algorithm, which utilizes the || operatorif (X > 0) || (Y < 7)  k = k + 1;endYou can restructure this algorithm using duplicate codeif (X > 1)  k = k + 1;else if Y_less_than_7()  k = k + 1;end...function result = Y_less_than_7()  coder.inline('never') %% don't inline  result = Y < 7;endAlternatively, you can avoid duplicating a large amount of code by using Boolean conditions.guard = false;  if (X > 1)    guard = true;  else if Y_less_than_7()    guard = true;  end  if guard    k = k + 1;  end...function result = Y_less_than_7()  coder.inline('never') %% don't inline  result = Y < 7;end

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Simulink Coder can sporadically generate zero-initialization code for bus signals and states
Description: If you initialize a signal or state with a MATLAB structure or a parameter object with a value that is a MATLAB structure, Simulink Coder might generate extra, but inconsequential, zero-initialization code for signals and states. In addition, this might have a secondary effect of generating an extra, but inconsequential, start function if the zero-initialization code occurs inside a subsystem start function.
Workaround: Set the initial condition parameter to 0 instead of using a MATLAB structure.In R2011a and earlier releases, for initializing bus signals containing elements with enumerated data types, use an initial condition structure that only specifies the fields corresponding to the elements with enumerated data types. All other fields will default to 0.

Summary: Gain block might fail to report overflow, or might report overflow when saturation occurs
Description: Suppose that your model contains a Gain block with the following characteristics: Input signal data type, "Output data type", and "Parameter data type" are the same. Input signal data type, "Output data type", and "Parameter data type" are floating point or any built-in integer type.  Then: If you select "Saturate on integer overflow" on the Gain block and overflow occurs during simulation, the block does not report the overflow. You do not see an error or warning, even if "Detect overflow" in the Signals group on the Diagnostics > Data Validity pane of the Model Configuration Parameters is set to "error" or "warning". If you select "Saturate on integer overflow" on the Gain block and overflow occurs during simulation, the resulting error or warning message reports saturation instead of reporting overflow.  ?
Workaround: None.

Summary: Specifying RTWData for inactive variant may cause segmentation violation
Description: If you specify the RTWData  parameter for a block that is part of an inactive variant, a segmentation violation may occur when you generate code for the model.
Workaround: If you intend to generate code for a model, do not specify the RTWData parameter for blocks that are inside inactive variants in the model.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect variable access in generated code for inlined variant subsystem
Description: Due to a potential error in the generated code for an inlined variant system, a situation can arise in which a variable of the same name is declared locally in both an Outputs  code block and an Update  code block, assigned a value in the Outputs  code, and then referenced in the Update  code as if it had a value. For example, consider the following annotated excerpt of the generated code for an inlined variant subsystem. Notice that: In the scope of the Outputs  code, rtb_Switch  is declared as a local variable and assigned a value. In the scope of the Update  code, rtb_Switch  is declared again as a local variable, not assigned a value, and then its value is accessed as an input.  When compiled, code of this form might get a warning, but no error, and when executed, might behave differently than the corresponding model simulation. void mymodel_step(void){#if variant_sub1    /* Outputs */    {       /* local block i/o variable */       real_T rtb_Switch;       if (mymodel_rtU.In1) > 4.0) {          rtb_Switch = 3.0;       } else {          rtb_Switch = mymodel_rtU.In1;       }       mymodel_rtB.Merge = rtb_Switch;   }#endif     /* variant_sub1 */   mymodel_rtY.Out1 = mymodel_rtB.Merge;#if variant_sub1    /* Update */   {       /* local block i/o variable */       real_T rtb_Switch;       /* Update for UnitDelay: 'S1/Unit Delay2' */       mymodel_rtDWork.Var1.UnitDelay_DSTATE = rtb_Switch;   }#endif     /* variant_sub1 */
Workaround: To work around this bug, take one of the following actions:Locate the code pattern that produces the issue in the generated code for your inlined variant subsystem. First, identify a variable that is declared and assigned in the scope of one code block, and for which a variable of the same name is declared but not assigned prior to use in the scope of a second code block. Next, identify the block whose output is represented by that variable, and then Testpoint that block output.Open the block dialog box for the affected subsystem, go to the Code Generation tab, and reset the Function packaging parameter from Inline to Function or Reusable function.
Products: Embedded Coder, Target Support Package, Embedded IDE Link

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Possible wrong answer from Discrete-Time Integrator block with ground non-scalar reset signal
Description: The generated code of a Discrete-Time Integrator block, with an external reset signal that is connected to a non-scalar Ground block, might produce a wrong answer.
Workaround: Do one of the following:Change the ground reset signal from non-scalar to scalar.Replace the Ground block with a Constant block.

Summary: Segmentation violation during AUTOSAR code generation for subsystem using Simulink.Signal object with non-auto data type
Description: Exporting functions from an AUTOSAR multi-runnable component might result in a segmentation violation if both of the following conditions exist: In the model, a signal representing an interrunnable variable resolves to a Simulink.Signal  object. (The error does not occur if the signal is located inside an exported runnable rather than between two runnables.) The Data type  value for the Simulink.Signal  object is set to a value other than auto .
Workaround: To work around this bug, take one of the following actions:Set the Data type value for the Simulink.Signal object to auto.Modify the model so that signals connecting runnables do not resolve to a Simulink.Signal object. Potentially, you could modify signals inside exported runnables to resolve to the Simulink.Signal object.

Summary: Compilation error for models containing signal objects of struct data type
Description: If your model contains a bus signal whose data type is set to struct , you may see an unclear error message when you compile the model.

Summary: Terminate function of a function-call subsystem called multiple times
Description: If a function-call subsystem has wide function call input (multiple callers) and the function calls are initiated by the Function-Call Generator block, then the generated code might call the terminate function multiple times.

Summary: Generating code multiple times for a model containing function-call Model blocks may produce different code, even when the model is not changing
Description: If a model contains multiple function-call Model blocks, then each time that you generate code for that model, the generated code may change the order of calls to certain functions in the referenced model for consecutive builds of the model. This can occur even when the model is not changing. The numerical results from the generated code are correct, even though the code has changed. For example, one portion of the generated code for one build may be: /* Model Initialize fcn for ModelReference Block: '<Root>/Asynchronous Counter' */   mr_sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_cntr_initialize(1, rtmGetErrorStatusPointer  (sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_M),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.AsynchronousCounter_DWORK1.rtm),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.AsynchronousCounter_DWORK1.rtdw));  /* Model Initialize fcn for ModelReference Block: '<Root>/500ms Counter' */  mr_sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_cntr_initialize(1, rtmGetErrorStatusPointer  (sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_M),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.u0msCounter_DWORK1.rtm),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.u0msCounter_DWORK1.rtdw));  /* Model Initialize fcn for ModelReference Block: '<Root>/1 sec Counter' */  mr_sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_cntr_initialize(1, rtmGetErrorStatusPointer  (sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_M),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.secCounter_DWORK1.rtm),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.secCounter_DWORK1.rtdw));  On a consecutive build, without changing the model, the code may change to the following. Note that now the blocks are called in a different order.  /* Model Initialize fcn for ModelReference Block: '<Root>/500ms Counter' */  mr_sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_cntr_initialize(1, rtmGetErrorStatusPointer  (sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_M),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.u0msCounter_DWORK1.rtm),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.u0msCounter_DWORK1.rtdw));  /* Model Initialize fcn for ModelReference Block: '<Root>/1 sec Counter' */  mr_sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_cntr_initialize(1, rtmGetErrorStatusPointer  (sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_M),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.secCounter_DWORK1.rtm),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.secCounter_DWORK1.rtdw));  /* Model Initialize fcn for ModelReference Block: '<Root>/Asynchronous Counter' */  mr_sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_cntr_initialize(1, rtmGetErrorStatusPointer  (sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_M),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.AsynchronousCounter_DWORK1.rtm),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.AsynchronousCounter_DWORK1.rtdw));

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Data consistency issues might occur when using the Minimize algebraic loop occurrences option on subsystem or model blocks
Description: Data consistency issues might occur when executing generated code if all of the following conditions are true: In your model, a multirate atomic subsystem or model block has a continuous sample time and one or more discrete subrate sample times. The block dialog option Minimize algebraic loop occurrences  is selected on the subsystem or model block. The subsystem or model block is involved in an artificial algebraic loop (AAL), which is being eliminated by Simulink. The model configuration parameter Tasking mode for periodic sample times  is set to Auto  or MultiTasking . The rate-based entry points of the generated code are executed in a preemptive multitasking environment.  Under these conditions, the persistent data associated with the discrete sample time blocks (for example, the DWork or BlockIO data) might be accessed from both the continuous (that is, base rate) task and the subrate task without data access (mutual exclusion) protection. This can cause data corruption during code execution.
Workaround: To help determine if the potential for this issue exists in code generated for your model, you can do the following:Identify at least one multirate atomic subsystem or model block in your model with continuous sample time and one or more discrete subrate sample times.Clear the Minimize algebraic loop occurrences option on subsystems and model blocks in the model.On the Diagnostics > Solver pane of the Configuration Parameters dialog box, set the Algebraic loop parameter to error.Simulate the model to determine if any artificial algebraic loops are detected by Simulink.If an artificial algebraic loop is detected, then condition 3 listed above in the Description section is true.Modeling based workarounds for this issue include any of the following:Avoid using both continuous sample time and discrete subrate sample times in the atomic subsystem or model block.Convert the atomic subsystem to a virtual subsystem.Break the artificial algebraic loop by inserting a delay block.Run-time based workarounds for this issue include any of the following:Avoid executing the generated model entry points in a preemptive multitasking manner.On the Solver pane of the Configuration Parameters dialog box, set the value of the Tasking mode for periodic sample times parameter to SingleTasking.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Data store in If or Switch block condition expression might produce incorrect results
Description: You might get incorrect generated code when all of the following conditions are met in your model: A data store is used in the condition expression of an If or Switch block. At least one of the branches contains a Data Store Write block, which writes to the data store used in the If or Switch block condition expression. In the generated code, each branch of the if  or switch  statement contains one and only one for  loop and the they have identical start, end, and stride values.  The attached model "mDataStoreInCond.mdl" illustrates the issue.
Workaround: Use a separate Data Store Memory block in the branches of the If or Switch block.
Products: Embedded Coder, Embedded IDE Link, Target Support Package

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Generated code for Selector block with multidimensional input and variable-size output
Description: The generated code of a Selector block with a multidimensional input might set incorrect dimensions of the resulting variable-dimension signal under all of the following conditions:   The multidimensional input signal includes one dimension of size 1, which is the third or higher dimension. For example, an input signal of size: [4][3][1][2] .   The Selector block parameter, Index Option , is set to Starting and ending indices (port)  on an input signal dimension of size 1. All subsequent dimensions from the dimension of size 1, have an Output Size  of 1.  For example, if the input signal is [3][4][1][4]  and the Selector block parameters are:   Input  1: Index Option  Starting index (dialog) ; Index  1; Output Size  2   Input  2: Index Option  Index vector (dialog) ; Index  1:3;   Input  3: Index Option  Starting and ending indices (port)    Input  4: Index Option  Starting index (dialog) ; Index  2; Output Size  1  The output signal is [2][3][1][1]  but Simulink omits the trailing 1's so that the actual output signal is [2][3] . The generated code is incorrect since all of the above conditions are met.
Workaround: Either of the following modifications generate the correct code:Option 1:For all input signal dimensions that are of size 1, specify the Selector block with Starting index(dialog), Index 1, and Output Size 1.  In the case of the previous example, make this modification to Input 3.Option 2:Make sure that at least one index along a dimension following the size 1 dimension selects a slice with output size greater than 1.  In the previous example, you can modify Input 4 to Starting index(dialog), Index 2, and Output Size 2, or Input vector (port) and Output Size 2.
Products: Simulink Coder, Target Support Package, Embedded IDE Link, Stateflow Coder

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Uncompilable code generated for a model with variant subsystems
Description: Simulink might generate uncompilable code for a model with a Variant Subsystem block under the following conditions: On the Variant Subsystem block parameter dialog, the Generate preprocessor conditionals  parameter is selected. On the Optimization  pane of the Configuration Parameters dialog box, the parameter Maximum stack size (bytes)  is set to a low value.  In this case, the generated code uses undeclared variables and does not compile.
Workaround: On the Optimization pane of the Configuration Parameters dialog box, increase the value of the Maximum stack size (bytes) parameter.

Summary: Simulation And Code Generation Mismatch: Interpolation Using Prelookup block might produce different results for simulation and code generation when fraction port data type is floating point and intermediate results data type is fixed point with messy slope and bias
Description: The Interpolation Using Prelookup block might produce different results for simulation and code generation when the following conditions are satisfied: The fraction port data type is floating point. The table data type is floating point. The intermediate results data type is fixed point, with messy slope and bias. The product of the table data and fraction data falls outside the range of the intermediate data type. Saturation on integer overflow is off.
Workaround: The simplest workaround is to set the intermediate results data type to be the same as the table data type.
Products: Target Support Package, Simulink Coder, Stateflow Coder, Embedded IDE Link

Summary: Referenced models with subsystem code variants and libraries may not rebuild properly when Rebuild is set to "If any changes in known dependencies"
Description: A referenced model will not automatically be rebuilt if all  of these conditions exist: Configuration Parameters > Model Referencing > Rebuild  for the top model is set to If any changes in known dependencies . The referenced model uses subsystem code variants. The only changes to that referenced model occur in libraries that are in inactive subsystem variants.
Workaround: Set Configuration Parameters > Model Referencing > Rebuild to If any changes detected.

Summary: Generated code can fail to compile when using variants with preprocessor conditionals
Description: The code generated for a model that uses variants with preprocessor conditionals can fail to compile if the variant references data of a custom storage class whose variable is defined in the code using the static keyword. The problem is caused by the static keyword preceding the preprocessor conditional, e.g. static #if UseSS2  real_T InBetSig = 0.0;#endif            when instead it should succeed the preprocessor conditional, e.g. #if UseSS2  static real_T InBetSig = 0.0;#endif            One example of such a storage class is 'FileScope', which may be used by mpt.Signal and mpt.Parameter objects.
Workaround: Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.Exit MATLAB.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. To see the contents of the .zip file: Windows Systems: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh):unzip -l <MATLAB>/<.zip file>Extract the files from the .zip file. Windows Systems: Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh): cd <MATLAB>unzip <.zip file>Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.Change log: May 12, 2011: attached attachment_726567_11a_2011-05-12.zip for 2011a only

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Possible wrong value of a for-loop index variable after last iteration.
Description: When generating code from a MATLAB algorithm that contains a for -loop, the index variable might be assigned a wrong value after the last loop iteration if all  of the following conditions are met: The for -loop is located inside an if  or switch  statement. The index variable is assigned outside  the loop from a constant value. The assignment occurs outside the if or switch statement, or in a different branch of it. The index variable of the for -loop is used  outside  of the loop.  This bug does not affect the for -loop, but subsequent use of the index variable might propagate an incorrect value. This bug occurs: In MATLAB Function blocks (formerly Embedded MATLAB Function blocks). When generating code from MATLAB using codegen  (formerly emlmex  and emlc ) and fiaccel  commands.
Workaround: Refactor the code so that the for-loop index variable is not used outside the loop.

Summary: Mux signals logged in Simulink.TsArray format have incorrect dimension when inside of Accelerator mode Model blocks
Description: When logging a mux vector using Simulink signal logging, incorrect sample dimension are logged if all of the following conditions exist: The signal is inside of a reference model. The Model block is in Accelerator mode. Configuration Parameters > Data Import/Export > Signal logging format  parameter is set to ModelDataLogs .
Workaround: In R2011a or later releases, set the Signal logging format parameter to Dataset.In earlier releases, set the Model block to Normal mode.

Summary: SegV when simulating a reference model that contains global data stores
Description: Simulating a top model that references models that access global data stores can cause a segmentation violation when both these conditions are met. The reference models are independent of sample time The parameter Periodic sample time constraint  for the reference models is not set to Ensure sample time independent
Workaround: If a reference model is sample-time independent, set its parameter Periodic sample time constraint to Ensure sample time independent.Note: To determine if a reference model is sample-time independent, set its parameter Periodic sample time constraint to Ensure sample time independent and then perform an update diagram. If the model is not sample-time independent, Simulink will generate an error.

Summary: Model block arguments are not evaluated correctly when the Model block is under a mask in a referenced model
Description: The model argument value of a Model block may not get evaluated correctly if all of the following are true: The Inline parameters  setting of the top model is set to On. There is a Model block under a mask that references a model that contains model arguments. This Model block lives in a referenced model that is referenced in Normal mode. One of the Model argument values in this Model block references a parameter from that mask.  In this case, Simulink does not push down the Model argument value from the Model block to the referenced model.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Generated code for a triggered Function-Call Subsystem block is not calling the terminate function
Description: The generated code for a Function-Call Subsystem block is not invoking the terminate function, except when the block is triggered by the Function-Call Generator block from the Simulink library. This issue exists in R2011a and R2010bSP1.

Summary: To Workspace logged variables may appear incorrect in a code reuse subsystem in Rapid Accelerator mode
Description: When you use Rapid Accelerator mode to simulate a model that has a To Workspace block inside a code reuse subsystem that is used multiple times in single model, then only 1 variable may be logged. When simulating in Normal mode, each To Workspace block instance logs a separate variable.
Workaround: Use one of the following approaches:Disable code reuse for the subsystem. In future Simulink releases, subsystems with To Workspace blocks will not reuse code. Simulate the model in Normal mode.

Summary: On Macintosh, MATLAB R2010b Service Pack 1 has a main menu that says "StartMATLAB", and emits warnings when launched from a terminal window. This issue does not show up in R2010b, only R2010b Service Pack 1.
Description: After MATLAB R2010b Service Pack 1 is launched on Macintosh, MATLAB's main menu says "StartMATLAB" instead of "MATLAB". In addition, when launched from the command line, MATLAB emits several messages that say: CFPropertyListCreateFromXMLData(): Old-style plist parser: missing semicolon in dictionary.  Both of these symptoms are benign and do not affect the proper functioning of MATLAB.
Workaround: Note that these instructions are for Macintosh only.First, log in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation.Then to fix the problems described above, you may either execute commands at the MATLAB command prompt or download and install a patch (choose one method or the other; you do not need to do both.)Option 1: Execute commands at the MATLAB command promptBe sure to copy these commands and paste them to the command prompt exactly as follows:cd (matlabroot);!perl -pi -e 's/" "//g' Contents/Resources/English.lproj/InfoPlist.stringsOROption 2: Download and install a patchLocate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.Exit MATLAB.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. To see the contents of the .zip file, type the following in a terminal window:unzip -l <MATLAB>/<.zip file>Extract the files from the .zip file by typing the following in a terminal window:cd <MATLAB>unzip <.zip file>Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.Change log:March 1, 2011: attachment_711742_10bSP1_2011-03-01.zip: initial version, works on R2010bSP1 only

Summary: Compiler warning or error for code generated from a subsystem connected to a root outport
Description: Compiling code generated for a subsystem generates a warning or error if all of the following conditions exist: The subsystem is configured to generate a nonreusable function. The subsystem is conditionally executed. For example, the subsystem is a triggered subsystem. The subsystem is connected to a root outport of the containing model. The containing model has been configured using Configure Model Functions  on the Interface  pane of the Configuration Parameters dialog box, such that the root outport to which the subsystem is connected is specified as the return argument of the model step function.  Under these conditions, compiling the generated subsystem code generates warning or error messages that might indicate type or pointer mismatches, for example,  "assignment from incompatible pointer type ".

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect reusable code generated for For Each subsystem whose input is driven by either a non-inlined S-function block or a block that inlines code using a TLC file
Description: The number of iterations of a For Each subsystem in the generated code is incorrectly initialized if the For Each subsystem block generates a reusable function and meets both  of the following conditions: The subsystem has an input port for which the For Each block dialog box has the Partition  parameter checked. This input port is driven by a non-inlined S-function block or a block that inlines code using a TLC file. Common examples of blocks with a TLC file for code generation are Model and S-function (C-Mex S-function, or a Level-1 or Level-2 MATLAB S-function) blocks.  Additionally, similar incorrect reusable code is generated for any For Each Subsystems that are nested within a For Each subsystem that satisfies the conditions above. To check whether this issue affects your model in the R2010a and R2010b releases, run the DetectForBugReport708786.p  script. See the Workaround  section below for details. ?
Workaround: Download the attached DetectForBugReport708786.p script to a folder and add the folder to the MATLAB path using the command addpath. Run the script on your model using the following syntax:ret = DetectForBugReport708786('modelname')For a model that is unaffected by this issue, the script returns an empty array. For an affected model, the script returns an array of structures with the following three fields:TopForEachSS: The For Each subsystem that meets the two conditions described in the Bug Description section above.TopForEachSSInput: The index of the input mentioned in the condition above.AffectedForEachSS: The full list of For Each subsystems including nested subsystems that are affected by the bug identified for the subsystem listed in TopForEachSSInput.Use one of the following two workarounds for the For Each subsystems in your affected model:For the For Each subsystems identified in TopForEachSS, place a Signal Conversion block in front of the input port marked in TopForEachSSInput. In the Block Parameters dialog box for the Signal Conversion block, set the Output parameter to Contiguous copy and check the Exclude this block from 'Block reduction' optimization parameter.For all For Each subsystems identified in AffectedForEachSS, set the Configuration Parameters > Code Generation > Real-Time Workshop system code parameter to Inline or Function.

Summary: MATLAB crashes when you select "Go To Library Block" menu item inside a masked subsystem in a library with none of its other blocks open in other windows
Description: MATLAB crashes when you select "Link Options > Go To Library Block" menu item on a linked block inside a masked subsystem, when the referenced block is also at the same level. This crash occurs when none of the parent blocks are open in other windows, for example when you open a library and navigate to the masked subsystem by clicking its node in the Model Explorer.
Workaround: To avoid this crash, use the keyboard shortcut instead. Press Ctrl+L, instead of using the right-click menu item "Link Options > Go To Library Block".

Summary: Simulink might crash when triggered or function-call subsystems have Inport blocks that are not sequentially numbered
Description: Inport blocks inside a subsystem or model should be numbered sequentially starting at 1. However, when constructing the model, you might have Inport blocks that are not sequentially numbered. For example, this situation might occur if you have only two Inport blocks, but their Port Number  values are 1 and 4. This non-sequential numbering results in the Subsystem block having 4 external ports instead of 2. When you perform update diagram on this model, you will see an error about the missing Inport blocks for ports 2 and 3. In R2010bSP1, R2010b, or R2010a, if a triggered or function-call subsystem is created with non-sequential Inport blocks as described above, an update diagram action causes a crash instead of an error. This behavior is illustrated in the attached model mTrigLatch_BadInportNum.mdl . You can check a model to see whether it is affected by this bug using the detectInportNumSkip_707514.p  function included in the attachment.
Workaround: As a workaround, to prevent this issue, ensure that Inport blocks in a triggered or function-call subsystem are sequentially numbered starting from 1 before you perform update diagram.

Summary: Empty Code Variants section of Code Generation report
Description: If you create a top model with variant model blocks, and some of those variant model blocks generate code variants, and other variant model blocks reference models that have function-call ports in their top diagram, then the Code Variants section of the Code Generation report is empty. This section should contain information about the code variants.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: The obsolete Lookup Table (2-D) block generates incorrect code for integer or fixed-point inputs when row and column index inputs have different kinds of evenly spaced data
Description: The obsolete Lookup Table (2-D) block generates incorrect code when the following conditions are met: (1) The inputs to the block are either integers or fixed-point data. (2) The parameters for Row and Column index input values are not tunable. (3) The parameter 'Row index input values' has power-of-two even spacing. (4) The parameter 'Column index input values' has even spacing, but not power-of-two even spacing.
Workaround: Because the Lookup Table (2-D) block is obsolete as of R2011a, you are recommended to use slupdate to replace it with the n-D Lookup Table block in the Simulink/Lookup Tables library.If you are using R2010a or later releases, you can use slupdate to upgrade the Lookup Table (2-D) block by following these steps:(1) Open your model that has the obsolete Lookup Table (2-D) blocks.(2) At the MATLAB command line, open slupdate by typing:  slupdate('modelname')(3) Slupdate prompts you to replace each obsolete Lookup Table (2-D) block.You can also fix the issue by manually modifying one of Simulink's source files. This fix works in any release. Please follow the steps below:(1) Enter the MATLAB root folder.(2) Then, go to the rtw/c/tlc/fixpt folder.(3) Open the file fixptlook2D.tlc using the MATLAB Editor.(4) In the file, search for the string:u0SearchMethod != searchEven(5) Go to the second occurrence of the string, and change 'u0SearchMethod' to 'u1SearchMethod'.(6) To verify this modification, download the attached Simulink model mLookupTable2D.mdl. The instruction for the verification is in the model.

Summary: Code generation might fail when source multiplies variable-size matrices
Description: MATLAB Coder might produce an error during code generation when the source performs an inner product operation with two variable-size matrices.
Products: Embedded IDE Link, Target Support Package

Summary: Simulation And Code Generation Mismatch: Software-In-the-Loop (SIL) and Simulink simulation mismatch for multi-rate subsystem
Description: A SIL block created for a subsystem might not reflect the same behavior under the following conditions The input signals to the selected Subsystem block are updated at multiple rates. The Inport blocks of the Subsystem block have the block parameter, Sample time  (for -1 inherited) , set to -1 .
Workaround: Avoid setting the block parameter, Sample time (-1 for inherited), to -1 for Inport blocks of subsystems intended for code generation.

Summary: Simulation And Code Generation Mismatch: If "Use integer division to handle net slopes that are reciprocals of integers" is on, a conversion between two fixed-point data types with different slopes and biases might cause a mismatch between simulation and code generation
Description: When performing a conversion between two fixed-point data types with mismatched slopes and biases, the simulation results might not provide bit-true agreement with the code generation results. Code generation provides the intended behavior. This problem can occur only under certain circumstances. The necessary conditions include the following: The "Use integer division to handle net slopes that are reciprocals of integers" option on the Optimization pane of the Configuration Parameters dialog box is selected. The two fixed-point data types have different biases. The two fixed-point data types have different slopes. The net slope correction is not a power of two. The net slope correction can be accurately represented as an integer reciprocal such as 1/30. The rounding mode specified for the cast matches the "Signed integer division rounds to" parameter setting configured for the embedded hardware on the Hardware Implementation pane of the Configuration Parameters dialog box.  Simulation incorrectly implements the decision logic to apply the "Use integer division to handle net slopes that are reciprocals of integers" optimization. Simulation incorrectly rules out all data type conversions involving mismatched bias.
Workaround: A workaround is to split the data type conversion into two conversions. In one conversion, change the bias, but not the slope. In the other conversion, change the slope, but not the bias. You can do these conversions in either order.

Summary: Simulink crashes when a model containing Simscape block in a subsystem is opened
Description: When a block in a custom Simulink library has a subsystem containing Simscape blocks, or blocks from add-on products, and the number of ports in this subsystem changes, opening a model containing that Simulink library block can cause Simulink to crash.

Summary: Bus objects with deeply nested hierarchies may cause block dialogs to become unresponsive
Description: If you have a block that contains a bus object with a deeply nested hierarchy, the block dialog for this block may become unresponsive when you open the dialog or apply changes to a field related to the bus object. You may observe that for a deeply nested hierarchy, the dialog becomes progressively more unresponsive after you apply several changes to a field related to the bus object.
Workaround: Remember: Use a workaround only if Simulink starts becoming progressively more unresponsive after you apply several changes to a field related to the bus object. Note: There is no workaround if the block dialog becomes unresponsive when you open it.If the dialog box becomes unresponsive when you are applying changes to a field in the dialog, you can use one of two possible workarounds:Save and close the model after applying each change. Then, reopen the model to make further changes.After each change, update the model by selecting Edit -> Update Diagram (Ctrl-D) in the Model Editor.

Summary: On Windows Vista, or Windows 7 with Aero features turned OFF, certain blocks may throw an error to the MATLAB command prompt when drawing.
Description: On Windows Vista, or Windows 7 with Aero features turned OFF, certain blocks (for example, Variable Transport Delay) may display the following error message in the MATLAB command prompt when drawn: "Windows returned error message: Not enough storage is available to process this command."  The issue is caused by the graphics system in Windows Vista and Windows 7, and the size of the block icon. This error happens when the icon gets small enough.
Workaround: Increase the size of the block icon for any block that causes this error.Alternatively, you can use a a different zoom level to view the model.

Summary: Closing a model after deleting a physical modeling subsystem can crash MATLAB
Description: Closing a model after deleting a physical modeling subsystem can crash MATLAB. This can occur if the subsystem contains a labeled connection line.
Workaround: Delete the contents of the subsystem.Save the model.Delete the subsystem.Save the model.Close the model.

Summary: Merge block with unconnected input port might cause segmentation fault during model compilation
Description: When a model contains a Merge block with an unconnected input port, model compilation generates a segmentation fault if one of the following two conditions is met: 1. The Merge block is used to merge the outputs of a set of If Action Subsystems 2. The Merge block is used to merge the outputs of a set of Enabled Subsystems
Workaround: Connect all input ports of the Merge block.

Summary: Simulink might not save and restore complete simulation state (SimState) for data with the nonvirtual bus data type
Description: If a Simulink model contains simulation state for data with the nonvirtual bus data type, Simulink might not correctly save and restore the complete SimState.
Workaround: As a workaround, do not use nonvirtual bus data types in the model.
